perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws in force in India.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws in force in India. Any dispute or difference arising out of or in connection with this Non- Discloser Agreement shall be governed by laws in force in United States.
perturbation.explanation: A clause is added to make the agreement subject to US law when it says the agreement is subject to Indian law, creating contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 28 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 addresses agreements in restraint of legal proceedings. The clause stating the agreement is subject to US law creates a legal contradiction, making it unclear which jurisdiction's laws apply. This ambiguity can impede the enforcement of the contract as the jurisdiction is a key component.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction by stating that the agreement is governed by both Indian and US law. This directly contradicts the purpose of a governing law clause, which is to clearly establish the jurisdiction whose laws will be used to interpret and enforce the contract. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight this contradiction. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly address contradictory governing law clauses, it does establish that Title 28 pertains to Judiciary and Judicial Procedure, which includes jurisdiction. This supports the idea that specifying jurisdiction is a key aspect of legal agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees not to disclose and the Discloser agrees to let the Recipient have the access to the Confidential Information as identified and reduced in writing or provided verbally or in any other way not reduced in writing at the time of such disclosure of the information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees not to disclose and the Discloser agrees to let the Recipient have the access to the Confidential Information as identified and reduced in writing or provided verbally or in any other way not reduced in writing at the time of such disclosure of the information. Recipient can disclose the information if they get written permission to do so.
perturbation.explanation: A clause is added to let the Recipient disclose information, which defeats the purpose of the Non-Disclosure agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 outlines the essential elements of a valid contract, including free consent and lawful object. By creating an exception within the confidentiality clause that directly contradicts the purpose of a Non-Disclosure Agreement, where the recipient can disclose with permission, renders the original condition of non-disclosure moot, casting doubts on its enforceability due to contradictory terms.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 18 / PART I / CHAPTER 1 / § 10 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 18 USC 10 : Interstate commerce and foreign commerce defined Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 18-CRIMES AND CRIMINAL PROCEDURE PART I-CRIMES CHAPTER 1-GENERAL PROVISIONS Jump To: Source Credit §10. Interstate commerce and foreign commerce defined The term "interstate commerce", as used in this title, includes commerce between one State, Territory, Possession, or the District of Columbia and another State, Territory, Possession, or the District of Columbia. The term "foreign commerce", as used in this title, includes commerce with a foreign country. ( June 25, 1948, ch. 645, 62 Stat. 686 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 18
perturbation.justification: The modified text adds a clause allowing the Recipient to disclose Confidential Information with written permission, directly contradicting the original NDA's purpose of non-disclosure. The Law Explanation suggests this creates doubt in the contract's enforceability. Snippet 1, while not directly relevant, highlights the importance of defined terms and commerce regulations, indirectly underscoring the need for clear and consistent contract terms. The added clause creates a structural flaw by introducing an exception that undermines the agreement's fundamental principle, fitting the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient acknowledges that disclosure or use of Confidential Information in violation of this Agreement could cause irreparable harm to Discloser including loss of lives and limbs of the persons and damage to the property, for which monetary damages may be difficult to ascertain or turn to be meaningless. The Recipient therefore agrees that Discloser will have the right, in addition to its other rights and remedies, to seek injunctive relief for violations of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient acknowledges that disclosure or use of Confidential Information in violation of this Agreement could cause irreparable harm to Discloser including loss of lives and limbs of the persons and damage to the property, for which monetary damages may be difficult to ascertain or turn to be meaningless. The Recipient therefore agrees that Discloser will have the right to seek injunctive relief for violations of this Agreement, but only if the Recipient consents to such an injunction.
perturbation.explanation: It introduces a requirement that the Recipient must consent to injunctive relief, undermining the Discloser's right to seek it. Injunctive relief is a critical component of NDA enforcement, and its availability should not be conditional on the breaching party's consent.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 36 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 discusses when injunctive relief may be refused. By making injunctive relief conditional on the Recipient's consent, the modified clause directly interferes with the court's ability to grant relief based on legal principles, which in turn, makes the NDA weaker.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the standard application of injunctive relief, which is designed to be unilaterally sought by the Discloser and granted by the court based on legal principles, not contingent on the Recipient's consent. By requiring the Recipient's consent for injunctive relief, the modified clause effectively nullifies the Discloser's right to seek such relief and undermines the court's authority to grant it, directly conflicting with the intention and purpose of injunctive relief in protecting confidential information. The snippets are not helpful in this case, but the perturbation explanation indicates that the change makes the NDA weaker and less effective by creating an unnecessary contingency that goes against normal practice.
